verses.commentary: Isaac thinks he's dying (but lives another 43 years, 35:28-29). Urgency motivates the blessing.
Time pressure creates the context for what follows.
Isaac's request sets the plan in motion.
The patriarchal blessing transfers covenant promises.
Rebekah eavesdrops and has time to act while Esau hunts.
She reveals the plan to Jacob.
This is not just paternal preference but covenant transmission.
Rebekah will orchestrate the deception.
The substitution plan begins.
Jacob will receive what God promised (25:23) but through deception.
Jacob worries about getting caught, not about the morality.
Jacob fears consequences, not sin.
Maternal authority overrides Jacob's hesitation.
The deception begins.
The disguise continues.
Elaborate deception with goatskins creating texture like Esau's hair.
The plan is complete.
He senses something.
Jacob claims to be someone he's not.
Invoking God in a lie. Compounding sin.
Isaac doubts.
Isaac is confused.
Deception succeeds.
Jacob commits fully to the deception.
The meal before the blessing.
Affection and final verification.
Convincing. The blessing begins.
Material blessing: heaven's dew, earth's richness, abundance.
This transfers covenant promises. Dominance over Esau. Echoes 12:3. The Abrahamic blessing is given.
The deception is complete just in time.
Esau arrives with legitimate expectation.
Truth this time. But too late.
The blessing cannot be revoked.
Anguish. Desperate plea.
Isaac names the sin but accepts the outcome.
Birthright taken (25:29-34), blessing taken.
The blessing exhausted.
Hebrews 12:17 says he found no place for repentance though he sought it with tears.
The opposite of Jacob's blessing. Barren existence.
Serve your brother. But when you grow restless (tarid, תָּרִיד), throw off his yoke. Edom (Esau's descendants) will periodically rebel against Israel.
Esau held a grudge (satam, שָׂטַם).
Rebekah hears of the murder plan.
Safety through flight to Laban in Mesopotamia.
Temporary exile expected—just some days until fury subsides.
Rebekah fears losing both sons: Jacob to Esau's violence, Esau to blood guilt.
Rebekah gives Isaac a reason to send Jacob away without revealing Esau's murder plot, appealing to the Hittite wives' effect (26:34-35).
